message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:p>
                  The global COVID-19 pandemic underscores the dire need of effective antivirals. Encouraging progress has been made in developing small molecule inhibitors targeting the SARS-CoV-2 RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (RdRp) and main protease (M
                  <jats:sup>pro</jats:sup>
                  ). However, the development of papain-like protease (PL
                  <jats:sup>pro</jats:sup>
                  ) inhibitors faces several obstacles. Nevertheless, PL
                  <jats:sup>pro</jats:sup>
                  represents a high-profile drug target given its multifaceted roles in viral replication. PL
                  <jats:sup>pro</jats:sup>
                  is involved in not only the cleavage of viral polyprotein but also modulation of host immune response. In this study, we conducted a drug-repurposing screening of PL
                  <jats:sup>pro</jats:sup>
                  against the MedChemExpress bioactive compound library and identified three hits, EACC, KY-226, and tropifexor, as potent PL
                  <jats:sup>pro</jats:sup>
                  inhibitors with IC
                  <jats:sub>50</jats:sub>
                  values ranging from 3.39 to 8.28 µM. The three hits showed dose-dependent binding to PL
                  <jats:sup>pro</jats:sup>
                  in the thermal shift assay. In addition, tropifexor inhibited the cellular PL
                  <jats:sup>pro</jats:sup>
                  activity in the FlipGFP assay with an IC
                  <jats:sub>50</jats:sub>
                  of 10.6 µM. Gratifyingly, tropifexor showed antiviral activity against SARS-CoV-2 in Calu-3 cells with an EC
                  <jats:sub>50</jats:sub>
                  of 4.03 µM, a 7.8-fold increase compared to GRL0617 (EC
                  <jats:sub>50</jats:sub>
                  = 31.4 µM). Overall, tropifexor represents a novel PL
                  <jats:sup>pro</jats:sup>
                  inhibitor that can be further developed as SARS-CoV-2 antivirals.
                </jats:p>
title: Drug repurposing screening identified tropifexor as a SARS-CoV-2 papain-like protease inhibitor
